query: definition and examples of cross-sectional study

retrieved_docs.title: Human Subjects Research Design -- Definition/Introduction -- Quantitative Research
retrieved_docs.content: A cohort study involves longitudinally following a group or groups of population with certain known exposures to determine who develops certain diseases or illnesses. This type of study could establish causal relationships between exposure and outcomes such as illness. [2] A cross-sectional study deals with a population at a given point in time as opposed to longitudinally and could provide information such as prevalence. Case-control studies compare populations with and without the exposure to determine if an illness will develop and at what rate in either group. A classic example is comparing smokers and non-smokers to determine which group develops lung cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Human Subjects Research Design -- Definition/Introduction -- Quantitative Research
retrieved_docs.content: For observational or non-interventional research studies, the investigator gathers data for identified variables of interest without any intervention or outside influence by the investigator on the groups under study. Cohort, cross-sectional, and case-control are the common types. [2]

Note that often j is used for current instead of I to prevent confusion with imaginary numbers. Therefore, one should pay attention to the symbols’ definitions, as they can vary case-by-case.

retrieved_docs.title: Human Subjects Research Design -- Definition/Introduction -- Mixed Methods Research
retrieved_docs.content: In some cases, a combination of both qualitative and quantitative methods, or what is called a mixed-methods research is performed. Mixed methods approaches that combine qualitative and quantitative research can allow for hypothesis generation and hypothesis testing to help try to answer questions in a more well-rounded way. This is usually done to get the benefits of both numerical and non-numerical information to answer the research questions on hand. For example, a cross-sectional study found that young teens are vaping at a high rate. For further elucidation of the reasons why these teens vape, a subsequent focus group could be performed.

retrieved_docs.title: Statistics -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Data management, on the other hand, employs the organization and analysis of health data. Data can be organized in numerous ways, so every researcher should only use methods depending on the specific goal of the study. For example, if the statistical data must be interpreted as individual units, it can be organized in the form of a raw data or data series (e.g., arranged in arrays or alphabetical order). This is usually done in studies having a small population (e.g., case studies, case series).  Otherwise, if the data needs to be described using frequency distribution, it can be organized either as discrete or continuous data series using frequency tables. This collection process is frequently used in studies with a larger study population. It is important to note that the best method of organizing statistical data primarily depends on the type of variable (e.g., qualitative or quantitative) and its level of measurement (e.g., nominal, ordinal, interval, ratio). Arriving at all possible data organizations may not be necessary if, and only if, this will give the best information to the researchers about the objectives of the study.

retrieved_docs.title: Double-Blind Study -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: A clinical research study or a clinical trial is an experiment or observation performed on human subjects to generate data on the safety and efficacy of various biomedical and behavioral interventions. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Gout -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Epidemiological estimates depend on the definition of the disease. A definitive diagnosis of gout is accepted in the presence of monosodium urate monohydrate crystals in the joint fluid or the identification of tophus. However, given the impracticality of identifying gout through these criteria alone, various case definitions have been devised, including self-reports, the Rome criteria, the New York criteria, the American College of Rheumatology (ACR) criteria, and the 2015 ACR/European League Against Rheumatism (EULAR) criteria. The 2015 ACR/EULAR criteria have a sensitivity of 92% and specificity of 89%, surpassing the accuracy of all previous definitions and ensuring a more precise and reliable diagnosis of gout in epidemiological studies.

retrieved_docs.title: Quality Of Life -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: QoL differs from the public health measure of health-related quality of life in that the latter is a measure that explores the connection between health and QoL. As there has been recognized difficulty with arriving at a universally accepted definition and measurement, recent studies have attempted to reframe QoL into separate domains. One example of this reframing is the "engaged theory," which takes QoL and divides it into 4 main domains: economics, culture, politics, and ecology. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Statistics -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The use of appropriate methods to organize data will lead to its accurate analysis. In descriptive data analysis, the use of narratives, tables, graphs, and charts can be sufficient to describe the study variables. In the inferential analysis, the researcher needs to either estimate specific clinical or health parameters or perform hypothesis testing. Several versions of data analysis software are available for use according to the type of research work. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Epidemiology Of Study Design -- Introduction -- Cohort Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Cross-sectional studies are observational in nature and give a snapshot of the characteristics of study subjects in a single point of time. Unlike cohort studies, cross-sectional studies do not have a follow-up period and therefore are relatively simple to conduct. As the exposure status/outcome of interest information is collected in a single moment in time, often by surveys, cross-sectional study design cannot provide a cause-effect relationship and is the weakest of the observational designs. This study design is generally used to assess the prevalence of a disease in a population. [5] Ecological Studies

retrieved_docs.title: Healthcare Analytics -- Definition/Introduction -- Types of Healthcare Analytics
retrieved_docs.content: Descriptive analytics: Descriptive analytics reviews historical data to provide an understanding of past or current events to identify meaningful patterns. [8] [10] Although not focused on causation, descriptive analytics helps healthcare organizations gauge overall experiences or outcomes through data visualization and illustration. [8] Descriptive analytics is the first step in addressing and identifying patterns and trends experienced in health care. Examples of descriptive analytics include cross-sectional analyses that describe a population at a specific time without analyzing cause and effect, graphs that display data, and some aspects of systematic reviews and meta-analyses that do not include root cause analyses. Healthcare professionals can use descriptive analytics to analyze processes needing improvement and gain insight into peak timing for common respiratory illnesses and populations with high rates of cardiovascular disease.

retrieved_docs.title: McNemar And Mann-Whitney U Tests -- Definition/Introduction -- McNemar test
retrieved_docs.content: The McNemar test is a non-parametric test used to analyze paired nominal data. It is a test on a 2 x 2 contingency table and checks the marginal homogeneity of two dichotomous variables. The test requires one nominal variable with two categories (dichotomous) and one independent variable with two dependent groups. Also, the two groups in the dependent variable must be mutually exclusive, i.e., cannot be in more than one group. The minimal sample size required for the McNemar test is at least ten discordant pairs. The formula for calculating the Chi-squared value for McNemar test appears in Image 1, where b is the false positive count, and c is the false negative count.

retrieved_docs.title: Epidemiology Of Study Design -- Introduction -- Observational Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Case-crossover studies are helpful to study triggers within an individual. When the researcher is studying a transient exposure or risk factor, the case-crossover design is useful. This is a relatively new study design where there is a case and a control component, both of which come from the same individual. Each case is self-matched by serving as its own control. Determining the control and case components period is a critical and difficult aspect of a case-crossover study. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The MMPI was developed in the 1930s and published through the University of Minnesota in 1942 by Stuart Hathaway and Charley McKinley using visitors of patients at the University of Minnesota hospital as a base sample in both theorizing constructs of psychiatric illness and fielding the instrument. Testing is administered through 567 true or false items using a booklet with an accompanying answer sheet. The responses are then hand-scored and plotted on an X-Y graph; a separate version is used for male and female respondents. [3] The X-axis is comprised of 14 scales. retrieved_docs.title: Psoriatic Arthritis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The Classification of Psoriatic Arthritis (CASPAR) study identified 63% of patients with polyarticular arthritis. [33] Axial disease is often associated with one of the patterns of peripheral arthritis. According to one prospective cross-sectional study, the frequency of radiological axial involvement in psoriatic arthritis was about 42.9%. [34] The distal pattern is less common, occurring in less than 20% of patients, and may be present with axial disease. Arthritis mutilans prevalence can range from 2% to 21%, reflecting different definitions of this entity adopted from various studies. [35]

This linkage clarifies the terminology used in multilingual clinical contexts and aids in consistent identification and communication of the condition across language barriers.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2675
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting an overlap in terminology where both terms describe the observable manifestations and patient‑reported experiences associated with poorly characterized medical disorders. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 964
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition is defined using the same diagnostic criteria as anxiety disorder due to known physiological condition, and these criteria are incorporated within the broader diagnostic framework for anxiety disorders. The entity Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere is classified under both mood disorders and anxiety disorders, reflecting its dual nature as a transient condition that can present with features of each category. Additionally, Transient organic psychotic condition, depressive type is closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, mood disorders, indicating that its depressive psychotic presentation is viewed as a variant within the mood‑disorder spectrum. Together, these relationships illustrate the overlapping boundaries among anxiety‑related, mood‑related, and transient psychotic conditions, emphasizing the importance of shared diagnostic criteria and cross‑categorical classification in clinical assessment and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are described as related to and possibly synonymous with the broader category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions.” Within this framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent entity “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that they are grouped under this overarching label. Likewise, malaise and fatigue is identified as a child of “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” linking this specific symptom complex to the same ill‑defined condition category. In addition to its hierarchical placement, malaise and fatigue is mapped to the Arabic term “النوام” and is also associated with the alternative Arabic expression “الضعف العصبي,” providing cross‑lingual equivalents for this clinical presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting the overlap between patient‑reported experiences and observable clinical manifestations in poorly characterized disorders. In this framework, diseases and injuries are placed under the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that many inadequately defined pathologies present with nonspecific clinical findings that are captured by this umbrella term. Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected nature of clinical presentations (symptoms) and the underlying, often vague, disease or injury processes, highlighting the importance of comprehensive evaluation when managing patients with ill‑defined conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 584
retrieved_docs.content: Signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions constitute a broad, overarching category that links together general symptoms, various diseases and injuries, and specific symptom clusters such as malaise and fatigue. The term “Symptoms” is described as closely related and possibly synonymous with this category, emphasizing that many nonspecific clinical presentations fall under the same conceptual umbrella. Within this framework, malaise and fatigue is identified as a child of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions and is mapped to the Arabic expressions “الشعور بالضعف” (feeling of weakness) and “النوام,” as well as to the related term “الضعف العصبي” (neurological weakness). Additionally, the condition “الانهيار في الدورة الدموية” (circulatory collapse) is listed as a child of the same category, illustrating that both mild, vague complaints and severe physiological failures are organized under the same ill‑defined signs and symptoms taxonomy. This hierarchical and multilingual mapping aids clinicians in recognizing how non‑specific complaints like malaise and fatigue relate to broader diagnostic categories and more serious manifestations, supporting comprehensive assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 821
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, the concept “burns” is closely linked to various types of injury. The relationship labeled “associated_with” connects “burns” to the Arabic term “جرح حروق” (burn wound), indicating that burn wounds are a primary manifestation of burns. Additionally, “burns” maintains a “has relationship” link with “حرق حراري” (thermal burn), specifying that exposure to heat is a common cause of burn injuries. The hierarchical notion expressed by “has child” ties the term “جرح حروق” to “حرق من سائل” (liquid burn), denoting that liquid burns are a subcategory or child type of burn wound. Together, these connections illustrate that burn injuries encompass thermal and liquid mechanisms, with burn wounds serving as the clinical presentation, and the relational descriptors “has child,” “has relationship,” and “associated_with” clarifying the structural and causal links among these concepts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1715
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral injury due to birth trauma is recognized as a specific manifestation of birth trauma, indicating that this type of brain injury occurs as a result of traumatic events during delivery. In medical terminology, the concept of birth trauma corresponds to the Arabic phrase “نزيف مخي وليدي,” linking the English description of traumatic birth‑related injuries to its equivalent expression in Arabic. This relationship underscores that cerebral injury caused by birth trauma is a subset of the broader category of birth trauma and that the term is directly mapped to its Arabic counterpart for cross‑linguistic clinical reference.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 155
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorders that arise from drug exposure or known physiological conditions are identified as اضطراب القلق بسبب حالة طبية عامة and are grouped with anxiety disorder due to another medical condition, sharing the same diagnostic criteria used for the broader class of anxiety disorders. These anxiety disorders are mapped to the miscellaneous mental health disorder group. Behavioral and psychiatric manifestations are placed under the parent category الهَذَيان (psychosis), which includes the child category حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة (transient organic psychotic conditions). Mental symptoms have a broader relationship with حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة and encompass specific sub‑symptoms such as probleem; persoonlijkheid (personality problem), فقد المتعة (anhedonia), and self‑destructive behavior (السلوك التدميري الذاتي). Anxiety disorders, miscellaneous mental health disorders, and mood disorders all classify حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة, reflecting overlapping classification across these diagnostic groups. Drug‑induced mood disorder is defined as a type of اضطراب الحالة المزاجية العضوي (organic mood disorder). The category “Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere” is positioned simultaneously under mood disorders, anxiety disorders, and miscellaneous mental health disorders, underscoring its cross‑cutting nature. The transient organic psychotic condition, depressive type is described as closely related to, and possibly synonymous with, mood disorders. Miscellaneous mental health disorders have a broader relationship with اضطراب نفسي عضوي عابر وغير محدد (unspecified transient organic psychotic disorder) and are primarily mapped from اضطراب الحالة المزاجية العضوي, indicating that many varied psychiatric conditions are understood to originate from or be classified under an organic mood disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2898
retrieved_docs.content: Mental disorder (اضطراب نفسي) is a broad diagnostic category that encompasses more specific conditions such as depression (اكتئاب) and separation anxiety disorder (اضطراب قلق الانفصال). Separation anxiety disorder is identified as a narrower form within the overall class of mental disorders, while depression is listed as a child concept of mental disorder, indicating its status as a distinct subtype. Epidemiology is recognized as an appropriate qualifier for mental disorder, depression, and separation anxiety disorder, underscoring the relevance of epidemiological research in describing the prevalence, distribution, and risk factors of these psychiatric conditions. This hierarchical organization and the emphasis on epidemiological data together support a comprehensive understanding of how these disorders are related and inform patient care and public‑health strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1522
retrieved_docs.content: The medical taxonomy places “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” as a distinct category that includes the specific manifestation labeled “غشي.” The term “Symptoms” is described as closely related and possibly synonymous with “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating substantial overlap in meaning. Moreover, the broader grouping “diseases and injuries” is identified as the parent of “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” positioning these vague clinical signs and symptoms as subcomponents within the larger domain of diseases and injuries. This hierarchical and synonymous relationship helps clinicians organize and interpret poorly defined clinical presentations within the overall framework of disease and injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 428
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition is defined using the diagnostic criteria established for anxiety disorder due to a known physiological condition, and these criteria are incorporated into the broader diagnostic framework for anxiety disorders. Within the classification system, the Arabic term “الذهان” (psychosis) includes a child category called “حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة” (transient organic psychotic conditions). Both anxiety disorders and mood disorders are described as classifying or encompassing these transient organic psychotic conditions, indicating that such conditions can be viewed under either anxiety‑related or mood‑related diagnostic groups. The category “Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere” is linked to both mood disorders and anxiety disorders as parent categories, reflecting its dual affiliation. Additionally, the specific entity “Transient organic psychotic condition, depressive type” is reported to be closely related to mood disorders and may even be synonymous with them. Together, these relationships illustrate the overlapping nature of certain transient psychotic and depressive presentations within both anxiety and mood disorder classifications, emphasizing the importance of recognizing shared diagnostic criteria and hierarchical links for accurate clinical assessment and treatment planning.
